metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The cooldown concurrency AC's observable oracle 'JWKS fake's request count is ≤1 for the burst' is satisfied by 0 fetches (no fetch at all), which would indicate the lock is preventing ALL fetches rather than capping to exactly one. The oracle should assert exactly 1 fetch (not merely ≤1) to distinguish correct behavior from a broken implementation that never fetches.
findings.location: AC: 'Unit test asserts unknown-`kid` cooldown is concurrency-safe: ... request count is ≤1 for the burst'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC text to: 'the stateful JWKS fake's request count equals exactly 1 for the burst (at least one fetch triggered, at most one — the asyncio.Lock-guarded check-and-update prevents TOCTOU double-fetch).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Security section whether the SSRF private-host guard resolves hostnames at construction time (to catch `https://internal.corp/jwks` → 10.x.x.x) or restricts only IP-literal URIs, and update the AC's observable oracle to include at least one hostname-as-private-IP test case so the chosen mechanism is actually exercised.
evidence: 'Private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback) are **rejected by default**'

AC oracle only specifies 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default and accepted only with `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true`' without prescribing whether hostname-based URIs (e.g. `https://internal.corp/jwks`) are resolved and checked
The AC test inputs are not listed — an executor writing only IP-literal tests satisfies the AC without covering hostname SSRF
findings.finding: The SSRF private-host guard is described as rejecting 'private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback)' but the detection mechanism is unspecified: the executor must independently choose between (a) IP-literal range check only, (b) DNS resolution at construction to catch hostname-based private addresses, or (c) both. These differ significantly in security properties and complexity — hostname-only URIs pointing to private IPs would bypass an IP-range-only check. The AC's observable oracle only exercises IP literals (`169.254.169.254`), so an IP-range-only implementation satisfies the AC while leaving hostname-based SSRF open.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch'; AC 'construction-time SSRF guards: ... private/link-local host is rejected by default'
scenarios: An attacker-controlled or misconfigured JWKS URI `https://internal-service.corp/.well-known/jwks.json` resolves to a RFC-1918 address but passes an IP-range-only check, enabling SSRF.
An executor implementing only IP-literal checks writes tests that pass the AC while leaving hostname-based private URIs unblocked.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Security section, e.g.: 'The private-host check is applied to the **resolved IP** of the JWKS URI hostname (synchronous DNS lookup at construction time via `socket.getaddrinfo`), not only to IP-literal URIs.' Then add to the SSRF AC: 'a `jwks_uri` whose hostname resolves to a private IP (e.g. a loopback hostname) is also rejected by default.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the Security / Approach section that the `asyncio.Lock` is acquired only for the timestamp check-and-stamp then released BEFORE the `await asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt())` call, so the lock is never held across I/O.
evidence: 'serializes the cooldown check-and-update with an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update, so two coroutines seeing the same unknown kid cannot both trigger a refetch'
Plan never states whether the Lock is held across `await asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt())` or released before it.

Timestamp-on-failure policy section confirms stamp happens on 'every fetch attempt (success OR failure)' — consistent with stamp-then-release-before-fetch, but this interpretation must be made explicit.
findings.finding: The asyncio.Lock scope during the unknown-kid refetch path is under-specified in a way that creates a real implementation trap: the plan says the Lock 'serializes the check-and-update' (timestamp only), implying it is released before the actual `await asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt())` call. But this is never stated explicitly. An implementer who instead holds the Lock across the `to_thread` await — a natural reading of 'serializes ... the check-and-update' to include the fetch — would serialize all concurrent `verify_token` calls during any JWKS refetch, creating a per-refetch throughput cliff under load. The correct design (stamp timestamp → release lock → run fetch in thread) must be made explicit.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' subsection; Approach & rationale — 'asyncio.Lock... serializes the cooldown check-and-update'
scenarios: Implementer holds Lock across the await asyncio.to_thread(...) call → all concurrent verify_token coroutines block behind the Lock during every JWKS refetch, serializing the verifier under load.
Implementer releases Lock before the to_thread call (correct) but an implementer reading only the Security section adopts the wrong interpretation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The Lock is acquired only long enough to read the last-refetch timestamp and write the new one (stamp-then-release); the actual `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` call runs outside the Lock so concurrent verification of cached-key tokens is never serialized.'

findings.finding: The asyncio.Lock boundary for the unknown-kid cooldown is ambiguous in a way that leads to a correctness trap: if the Lock is held across `await asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt)`, it serializes ALL concurrent token validations for the full HTTP timeout duration (up to 10 s), creating head-of-line blocking. If the Lock is released before the fetch (only protecting the timestamp update), then multiple concurrent to_thread calls for the same unknown kid proceed to call PyJWKClient concurrently — racing on PyJWKClient's internal cache dict, which is not documented as thread-safe. The plan's phrase 'serializes the check-and-update' is insufficient to specify which boundary is correct.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' paragraph; also Approach & rationale — 'asyncio.Lock (see Security) so the at most one fetch per cooldown window guarantee holds'
findings.suggested_fix: The recommended pattern: (1) acquire Lock → check cooldown → update timestamp → release Lock → call await asyncio.to_thread(fetch_and_update_jwks_cache) outside the Lock. For thread-safety on cache hits, either document that PyJWKClient's internal dict is protected by the GIL (CPython-only assumption, acceptable but worth noting), or wrap PyJWKClient in a per-instance threading.Lock for all get_signing_key calls. Alternatively, hold the Lock across the to_thread call only when a refetch is needed, and use a separate (non-locking) path for cache-hit known-kid lookups.

findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp`→scopes dual-claim mapping is specified as a feature but the decision rule for when both claims are present simultaneously is never defined. An executor does not know whether to take `scope` first, `scp` first, or merge them (union/intersection), and this rule is not co-located with the `AccessToken.scopes` output description.
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'

findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp`→scopes claim-mapping protocol is referenced in the What section but the merge/priority rule for when BOTH claims are simultaneously present in a JWT is never defined. This is a missing decision rule for a named processing protocol (T8b).
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Claims are resolved in order: `scope` (space-delimited string) is checked first; if absent, `scp` (space-delimited string or list) is used; if both are present, `scope` takes precedence and `scp` is ignored.'

findings.finding: The asymmetric algorithm allowlist is expressed as wildcard families (`RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA`) but the exact closed string set is never enumerated. An executor cannot implement the startup-refusal check or the `algorithms=` allowlist from wildcards alone — they must independently decide which specific strings (e.g. RS256/RS384/RS512, PS256/PS384/PS512, ES256/ES384/ES512, EdDSA) are valid, making the allowed set ambiguous and untestable.
findings.location: Security section — 'anything outside the asymmetric families RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA on a JWKS source is likewise refused'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'anything outside the asymmetric families `RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA`' with an explicit closed list: `{RS256, RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, EdDSA}` (case-sensitive, matching PyJWT's algorithm name strings).

findings.finding: The closed set of accepted asymmetric algorithm strings is specified only as wildcard families ('RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA') but the exact strings an executor must allow are never enumerated. The symmetric-rejection set IS explicitly enumerated ({HS256, HS384, HS512}), but the allowed-asymmetric side is not — an executor must independently decide whether 'RS*' includes RS256/RS384/RS512/RS1 and whether 'PS*' includes PS256/PS384/PS512. This is a vocabulary-completeness gap (T8a).
findings.location: Security section — 'refuse to start if any symmetric algorithm is configured … The symmetric set that triggers the startup refusal is the closed, explicitly-enumerated family {HS256, HS384, HS512} (case-insensitive); anything outside the asymmetric families RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA on a JWKS source is likewise refused.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'The closed asymmetric allowlist is: RS256, RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, ES256K, EdDSA (matching PyJWT's supported asymmetric algorithms); any configured algorithm outside this set OR inside {HS256, HS384, HS512} causes startup refusal.'

findings.finding: The cooldown state-increment placement for the `asyncio.Lock`-guarded last-refetch timestamp is not specified: the plan says 'check-and-update' but never states whether the timestamp is written *before* the JWKS fetch (preventing concurrent fetches but charging the cooldown on failure) or *after* the fetch (allowing concurrent fetches if two coroutines read the stale timestamp before either writes). This placement is load-bearing for both the correctness guarantee and the AC 8 observable oracle.
findings.location: Security section — 'it is therefore made atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update'; Approach section — 'serializes the cooldown check-and-update with an asyncio.Lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'The timestamp is updated inside the Lock *before* dispatching `asyncio.to_thread(...)` so concurrent coroutines see the updated mark and skip the fetch. A failed fetch does NOT reset the timestamp — the cooldown window remains charged to prevent retry storms.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC item 3) that explicitly tests a JWT carrying an `scp` claim (in addition to `scope`) maps correctly to `AccessToken.scopes`.
evidence: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' — What section
AC item 3 only says 'an AccessToken with the expected scopes' with no mention of the scp claim path
ABSENCE: no AC specifies a JWT carrying an `scp` claim (rather than `scope`) as a test case
findings.finding: The What section explicitly describes 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' as a deliverable behavior (both claim names), but no AC tests the `scp` claim variant — only AC item 3 checks 'expected scopes' generically without an oracle for `scp`.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes') vs. Acceptance Criteria item 3
scenarios: A JWT from Azure AD / Entra ID or other providers that use `scp` instead of `scope` passes implementation silently without coverage; a regression removing `scp` handling would not be caught by the acceptance suite.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC item 3: 'and that a JWT carrying `scp` instead of `scope` also maps to the expected scopes (observable oracle: AccessToken.scopes equals the scp array).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the timestamp-on-failure policy: when a JWKS fetch fails, a subsequent unknown-kid request within the cooldown window does NOT trigger another fetch attempt (observable oracle: fake JWKS server request count remains 1 after a failure + within-window retry).
evidence: 'the last-refetch timestamp is updated on every fetch *attempt* (success OR failure), not only on success, so a JWKS endpoint that errors cannot be used to bypass the flood guard' — Security section
ABSENCE: no AC specifies a test where a JWKS fetch fails and confirms that a subsequent unknown-kid request within the cooldown window is still suppressed (not re-attempted)
findings.finding: The Security section specifies 'the last-refetch timestamp is updated on every fetch attempt (success OR failure)' as an explicit security property preventing bypass of the flood guard, but no AC verifies this timestamp-on-failure behavior.
findings.location: Security section ('Timestamp-on-failure policy') vs. Acceptance Criteria (all items)
scenarios: If an implementer updates the timestamp only on success, a continuously-failing JWKS endpoint would allow unbounded refetch attempts, defeating the flood guard. No AC would catch this regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the JWKS fetch is offloaded via asyncio.to_thread (observable oracle: e.g., patch asyncio.to_thread and assert it is called during token verification, or demonstrate a concurrent task is not starved during a slow fake JWKS response).
evidence: 'The verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...)' — Approach section
ABSENCE: no AC specifies a test that confirms the event loop is not blocked (e.g., verifying asyncio.to_thread is invoked, or that a concurrent task is not starved during a slow JWKS fetch)
findings.finding: The Approach section specifies a concrete architectural requirement — 'offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via `asyncio.to_thread(...)` so it never blocks the event loop' — but no AC verifies that the blocking call is actually offloaded rather than called directly in the coroutine.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('Async-safety of the blocking JWKS fetch') vs. Acceptance Criteria (all items)
scenarios: An implementer could call PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt() directly in the coroutine body. All functional ACs would still pass (tokens validate correctly), but the event loop would block under load — the stated async-safety guarantee would be silently absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-level requirement (and a corresponding AC) that JWKS fetch failures emit a structured, redaction-safe log event at WARNING or ERROR level (e.g. `logger.warning('jwks_fetch_failed', uri=..., error_type=...)`) so operators can distinguish endpoint outages from token validation errors without exposing token material.
evidence: 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret substring is ever logged' — specifies only what is suppressed, not what is emitted.
No metric, structured log event (e.g. logger.warning with a stable event key), or alerting hook is mentioned for JWKS HTTP errors, timeout expiry, or key-not-found failures.
ABSENCE: no AC line requires an observable log event or metric for the JWKS failure path.
findings.finding: JWKS fetch failures are a new external-dependency failure point but the plan specifies no observability instrumentation (structured log event, metric counter, or alert) for them — only that error messages are redacted via the S2 helper. Operators cannot distinguish a JWKS endpoint outage from a misconfigured issuer or a token validation error without explicit instrumentation.
findings.location: Security section — 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)'; also the final bullet 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper'
scenarios: JWKS endpoint goes down; all JWT-authenticated requests start returning 401; operator has no log signal distinguishing this from bad tokens and cannot diagnose without a debug session.
A misconfigured auth_jwt_jwks_uri silently fails at startup or first use; no structured error surface guides the operator to the misconfiguration.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC: 'A JWKS fetch failure (HTTP error, timeout, or key-not-found) emits a structured log event at WARNING level with a stable `event` key (e.g. `jwks_fetch_failed`), the JWKS URI, and the error class — but never the raw token or any key material (routed through the S2 redaction helper).' This is purely additive and does not change the fail-secure behaviour.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC testing the `auth_jwt_leeway` behavioral contract: a token expired within the configured leeway window is accepted, and the same token is rejected when leeway is set below the elapsed expiry delta (observable oracle: accept vs. reject based on the configured leeway value).
evidence: AC4 enumerates rejection cases: 'wrong iss; aud ≠ auth_resource_server_url; expired exp; nbf in the future; alg:none; and an HS256 token...' — leeway boundary is absent
The What section names `auth_jwt_leeway (seconds, default 60)` as a first-class config key
No AC reads: 'a token expired within leeway is accepted; a token expired beyond leeway is rejected'
ABSENCE: no test that mints a JWT with exp=now-30s and verifies it is accepted with leeway=60 and rejected with leeway=0
findings.finding: The `auth_jwt_leeway` configuration parameter (named, security-relevant, default 60 s) has no acceptance criterion verifying its behavioral contract: that a token expired within the leeway window is accepted, and a token expired beyond the window is rejected. AC4 tests that an expired token is rejected, but never inverts the scenario with a non-zero leeway to confirm the parameter is wired and functional. A bug that ignores the leeway config (e.g., hardcodes 0 or a fixed value) would not be caught by the current test suite.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (expired `exp` negative control) and What section (`auth_jwt_leeway`, default 60)
scenarios: An operator configures leeway=0 for strict validation; a bug ignores this and uses the hardcoded default — no test catches it
A refactor accidentally removes the leeway= argument to jwt.decode — the expired-exp rejection test still passes (using default leeway=0 in PyJWT), masking that the intended default of 60 s is lost
A clock-skewed legitimate client whose token expired 45 s ago should be accepted with the default leeway of 60 s — untested
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'Unit test asserts `auth_jwt_leeway` is honored: a JWT with `exp = now − (leeway/2)` is accepted when leeway is at its default (60 s), and rejected when `auth_jwt_leeway=0` is configured; observable oracle: accept vs. reject outcome for the same token under different leeway settings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact `PyJWKClient` constructor signature for the installed `pyjwt` version (e.g., via `python -c "import inspect, jwt; print(inspect.signature(jwt.PyJWKClient))"`) and update the plan with the confirmed parameter name and passing mechanism before implementation.
evidence: Plan quote: 'passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient` so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier'
resolve_symbol('PyJWKClient', 'jwt') → EXISTS, but constructor signature is not readable from the repo
PyJWT's PyJWKClient historically used `lifespan` for cache TTL; timeout passing mechanism is library-version-specific
findings.finding: The Security section asserts the JWKS HTTP timeout is enforced by passing it 'as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`'. `PyJWKClient` exists in the installed environment, but the specific constructor keyword `timeout=` is unverifiable from the repo (the venv source is outside the repo root). PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` historically accepted `lifespan` (cache TTL) and the HTTP timeout was applied to the underlying `urllib` request, not necessarily as a direct `timeout=` constructor parameter. If the kwarg name or passing mechanism differs from the plan's assertion, the timeout will silently not be applied and the SSRF guard is incomplete.
findings.location: Security section: 'a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default `10`), passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`'; AC item 7: 'the JWKS client is constructed with `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default `10` s)'
scenarios: PyJWKClient in the installed version does not accept `timeout=` as a constructor kwarg; the timeout is silently ignored, leaving the verifier vulnerable to a slow-JWKS DoS.
The timeout must be passed to the underlying HTTP client (e.g., `headers` dict or request object) rather than the constructor, requiring a different implementation approach.
findings.suggested_fix: If `timeout=` is not a direct constructor parameter, the plan should specify the alternative (e.g., wrapping the fetch in a custom `requests.Session` with a timeout, or using `PyJWKClient`'s `get_signing_key_from_jwt` with a patched session).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and `docs/env-vars.md` to the edit-target list, and include regenerating `docs/env-vars.md` (alongside `docs/mcp-reference.md`) as an explicit step in the Acceptance Criteria.
evidence: Plan says 'regenerates the reference docs' without enumerating docs/env-vars.md
scripts/gen_env_registry.py scans src/rebar for os.environ reads and generates docs/env-vars.md
CI drift gate: test.yml line 260: `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`
gerrit-verify.yaml line 219: `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`
ABSENCE: neither gen_env_registry.py nor docs/env-vars.md appears anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The plan says it 'regenerates the reference docs' but only implicitly covers `docs/mcp-reference.md` (via `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py`). A second generated doc, `docs/env-vars.md`, is maintained by `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`, which scans `src/rebar` for env reads. Any `os.environ.get('AUTH_JWT_*')` / `os.getenv(...)` calls added to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` will cause the `gen_env_registry.py` CI drift gate (run in both `test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml`) to fail unless `docs/env-vars.md` is also regenerated. Neither `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` nor `docs/env-vars.md` is named in the plan.
findings.location: What section: 'This story registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json + regenerates the reference docs'
scenarios: An implementer adds AUTH_JWT_* os.environ reads in _mcp_auth.py, runs make test, and the env-vars registry drift gate fails because docs/env-vars.md was not regenerated — a CI break not anticipated by the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC item 2, append: 'and `docs/env-vars.md` is regenerated via `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` (the env-vars registry drift gate also passes in CI).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact `timeout` constructor kwarg name accepted by the installed `PyJWKClient` version (e.g. `python -c 'import inspect, jwt; print(inspect.signature(jwt.PyJWKClient.__init__))'`) and update the plan (and AC) with the verified kwarg name, or document a fallback if the kwarg is not supported.
evidence: Plan quote: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default `10`), passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient` so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier'
Cached review entries ce395984, cfa981eb, 87aab5f0 all flag this as unverified
jwt/jwks_client.py is outside repo root and cannot be read directly to confirm the kwarg name
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`' as a verified implementation capability, but the `timeout` constructor kwarg on `PyJWKClient.__init__` is not directly verified in the plan and cannot be read from the installed package file (outside repo root). Multiple prior review cache entries independently flag this as unconfirmed.
findings.location: Security — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' / 'passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient` so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier'
scenarios: PyJWT's PyJWKClient uses a different kwarg name (e.g. `request_timeout` or none at all on older versions) — the executor silently drops the timeout, leaving the verifier vulnerable to JWKS endpoint stalls
The AC test 'the concrete timeout value is passed to the client' passes via mocking but never exercises the real kwarg, masking the mismatch
findings.suggested_fix: Run `python -c 'import inspect, jwt; print(inspect.signature(jwt.PyJWKClient.__init__))'` against the target environment. If the kwarg is `timeout`, the plan is correct. If it is absent or named differently, adjust the implementation note to use the correct interface (e.g. wrap the underlying `urllib.request` call or pass a custom `ssl.SSLContext`/request factory).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a floor pin for `pyjwt[crypto]` in `pyproject.toml` (e.g. `pyjwt[crypto]>=2.6`) to guarantee the `timeout=` constructor kwarg exists in `PyJWKClient`, and update AC item 7's test oracle to verify the HTTP timeout is *applied* (e.g. inspect `PyJWKClient._jwks_uri` fetch behavior or check the `requests.Session` timeout) rather than only asserting the kwarg was passed at construction.
evidence: Plan: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key auth_jwt_jwks_timeout (seconds, default 10), passed as the timeout= argument to PyJWKClient'
AC item 7: 'the JWKS client is constructed with timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout (default 10 s) (observable oracle: ... the concrete timeout value is passed to the client)'
pyproject.toml mcp extra: only 'mcp>=1.9' — no PyJWT floor pinned
resolve_symbol(jwt.PyJWKClient): EXISTS, but source file path inaccessible in snapshot — exact constructor signature UNVERIFIED
Evidence class: Partially-verified
findings.finding: The plan asserts `PyJWKClient(timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout)` as the mechanism for bounding JWKS HTTP fetches — a security-critical guarantee. AC item 7 makes this a verifiable oracle ('the concrete timeout value is passed to the client'). The `timeout=` constructor kwarg exists in PyJWT ≥ 2.6.0, but the installed PyJWT version is not pinned in pyproject.toml (only `mcp>=1.9` is declared, which carries PyJWT transitively). The kwarg's existence in the exact installed version is Partially-verified: the source file path in the snapshot is inaccessible. If the installed version predates the `timeout` kwarg, the AC test will pass (the kwarg is accepted by `**kwargs`-style constructors) while the timeout is silently not applied — leaving unbounded JWKS fetches despite a passing test suite.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC item 7 — 'the JWKS client is constructed with `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout`'
scenarios: An environment where mcp>=1.9 resolves to a version that pulls PyJWT < 2.6 lacks the timeout= kwarg; the kwarg is silently ignored and the JWKS fetch has no timeout bound, contradicting the stated security guarantee.

The AC test for 'concrete timeout value is passed to the client' may pass by introspecting a constructor argument rather than verifying it is actually applied as an HTTP timeout, giving a false green.
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml`, change the `[mcp]` extra to `["mcp>=1.9", "pyjwt[crypto]>=2.6"]`. In AC item 7, tighten the oracle: instead of only checking the kwarg was passed, instantiate the verifier and confirm the underlying HTTP client carries the expected socket timeout (e.g., by monkeypatching `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt` and asserting the client's `session.get_adapter('https://').timeout` equals the configured value).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the SSRF private-host guard is enforced at **fetch time** on the resolved IP address (not only at construction on the hostname string) — e.g., by wrapping PyJWKClient's HTTP layer or post-resolving the hostname via `socket.getaddrinfo` before each fetch — so DNS rebinding cannot bypass the guard after construction.
evidence: Plan: 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: `jwks_uri` is fixed at construction from trusted config (never the token — no `jku`/`x5u`), HTTPS-only (an `http://` URI is rejected at construction), with a bounded HTTP timeout ... Private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback) are rejected by default'
The plan's SSRF guard is entirely described as a construction-time check on the URI/hostname string — no fetch-time resolved-IP validation is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
PyJWKClient (EXISTS: jwt.PyJWKClient in installed env) performs HTTP requests via urllib/requests at fetch time, resolving DNS independently from the construction-time hostname inspection.

AC7: 'Unit test asserts construction-time SSRF guards: an http:// jwks_uri is rejected; a private/link-local host is rejected by default' — the AC itself is scoped to construction-time, confirming no fetch-time guard is planned.
findings.finding: The SSRF private-host guard is performed only as a hostname-string check at construction time, but PyJWKClient resolves DNS independently at each HTTP fetch (not at construction). A DNS rebinding attack — or an internal DNS zone that maps a public-looking hostname to a private IP — can cause the actual JWKS HTTP request to reach a private/link-local address (e.g. 169.254.169.254, RFC 1918) even though the hostname string passed the construction-time check. The plan never specifies fetch-time IP validation, leaving the SSRF guard bypassable via DNS after construction.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: `jwks_uri` is fixed at construction ... Private/link-local hosts ... are rejected by default'
scenarios: An attacker who can influence the operator's DNS (e.g., via BGP hijack, split-horizon DNS misconfiguration, or a DNS rebinding attack in a cloud environment where the metadata endpoint is at 169.254.169.254) causes the JWKS fetch to hit the cloud instance metadata service, potentially exfiltrating IAM credentials.

An operator with a legitimate internal IdP sets auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true, then the IdP's public FQDN is briefly hijacked to resolve to a different private IP — the opt-out provides no protection in this case either.
findings.suggested_fix: After each JWKS HTTP response is received (or before the request, via a pre-fetch socket-level resolve), validate the resolved IP against the same private/link-local block-list using `ipaddress.ip_address(resolved_ip).is_private` / `.is_link_local` / `.is_loopback`. Since PyJWKClient does not natively support a pre-request IP hook, the simplest approach is to pre-resolve the hostname with `socket.getaddrinfo` before calling `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` and raising if any resolved address is in a blocked range. This pre-resolve guard runs inside the `asyncio.to_thread` call so it does not block the event loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` has a concrete operator scenario requiring tuning beyond the 30s default; if not, consider making it a private constant rather than a public env var to avoid config-surface proliferation.
evidence: 'auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown (seconds, default 30)' listed as a public flat config key
Security section frames the cooldown as an internal anti-DoS invariant: 'caps refetch on an unknown kid to at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds'
No operator scenario cited where 30s is wrong (no AC tests different cooldown values, no 'operators with fast-rotating keys' use case mentioned)
AC item 2 requires it registered in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json and reference docs — permanent public surface
findings.finding: `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` is exposed as a public, operator-visible flat env key (registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appearing in reference docs), but it is a purely internal flood-guard implementation detail — the cooldown window controlling TOCTOU protection on JWKS refetch. The plan cites no operator scenario where the 30s default is inadequate, and the Security section's own framing ('at most one fetch per cooldown window') presents it as a fixed implementation invariant rather than a tunable. Leaking it as a public config key adds surface without a current use-case.
findings.location: What section (config keys list) / Security section ('Key rotation without DoS') / AC item 2 ('registered in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json')
scenarios: Operator documentation grows a knob with no guidance on when/why to change it, increasing cognitive load and support burden.
A future maintainer changes the default without understanding the security invariant it encodes, weakening the flood guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `FastMCP`'s `JWTVerifier` (or its current equivalent) exists in the installed `mcp` SDK and update the Approach section with its correct module path, or remove the reference if it no longer exists.
evidence: Plan quote: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
findings.finding: The plan states 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as design-validation prior art for the chosen `PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode` architecture, implying `JWTVerifier` exists in the installed `mcp` SDK. Resolution probes of `mcp.server.fastmcp.JWTVerifier`, `mcp.server.auth.JWTVerifier`, and `mcp.JWTVerifier` all returned UNRESOLVED. The rationale is thus unverifiable and, if wrong, the implementation loses its design prior-art anchor — and the team may miss SDK-native verification utilities or API constraints.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: The SDK may have renamed or moved the verifier class, making the design prior-art reference obsolete.
An executor following 'the same shape' may adopt a non-existent interface contract, leading to a mismatch with the actual SDK seam.
findings.suggested_fix: Run `python -c "import mcp; print([x for x in dir(mcp) if 'jwt' in x.lower() or 'verify' in x.lower()])"` across the `mcp.server.*` namespace to locate the actual SDK class, then reference it by its confirmed fully-qualified name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Approach rationale: replace 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' with 'PyJWT is present in the installed environment as a transitive today, but is not pinned in our declared dependency graph; the explicit `pyjwt[crypto]` add to `[mcp]` is therefore mandatory, not optional.'
evidence: pyproject.toml line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` — no PyJWT declared
Plan quote: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp; pyjwt[crypto] makes the cryptography backend explicit for RS/ES verification'
jwt.PyJWKClient resolves in the installed environment (confirming it is present today as a transitive), but this is a runtime fact not guaranteed by the declared dependency graph
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' to rationalize that `pyjwt[crypto]` is a safe, backward-compatible add to the `[mcp]` extra. Probing `pyproject.toml` shows `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` — PyJWT is not declared as a direct dependency of this package, and its presence as a transitive today depends entirely on `mcp>=1.9`'s own undeclared dependency tree. If `mcp` drops or replaces PyJWT in a future release, the `[mcp]` extra will lose the library without any pin or explicit dependency. The plan's own mitigation (adding `pyjwt[crypto]` explicitly) is correct and makes the claim moot for correctness — but the premise stated in the plan is fragile and could mislead a reader into thinking the explicit add is optional.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the `cryptography` backend explicit'
scenarios: A future `mcp` release drops PyJWT as a dependency; the `[mcp]` extra silently loses JWT validation unless `pyjwt[crypto]` is pinned explicitly — which the plan does, making the AC correct but the rationale misleading.
A reader interprets 'already a transitive' as meaning the explicit AC add is redundant and omits it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `JWTVerifier` (or its equivalent) exists in the installed `mcp` SDK version and cite its fully-qualified path, or replace the reference with the actual interface/base class the verifier must implement (e.g. the S2-defined verifier protocol/ABC).
evidence: Plan text: 'giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam. PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp; pyjwt[crypto] makes the cryptography backend explicit ... the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
findings.finding: The Approach section justifies the design by saying it follows 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses', implying implementers should use this as a reference implementation. However, `JWTVerifier` is UNRESOLVED in every probed mcp namespace (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`). If it does not exist in the installed SDK version, implementers have no prior-art reference to consult, and the design rationale is ungrounded.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: Implementer tries to locate FastMCP's JWTVerifier as a shape reference and cannot find it in the installed SDK, leaving them without the stated prior-art anchor for the design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `lifespan` value passed to `PyJWKClient` (e.g., set `lifespan=0` to disable library-level TTL expiry, relying solely on the verifier's own cooldown for refetch scheduling) and clarify whether the single-fetch-per-cooldown guarantee covers lifespan-triggered refetches.
evidence: 'PyJWKClient's lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard, so this verifier implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself'
Plan does not specify what `lifespan` value is passed to PyJWKClient, or that lifespan-triggered refetches are also bounded.

Under concurrent `asyncio.to_thread` calls, each runs in a separate OS thread competing on PyJWKClient's internal threading.Lock, which may or may not serialize JWKS fetches depending on the PyJWT version.
findings.finding: PyJWKClient's own `lifespan` TTL-based cache expiry can trigger JWKS refetches at the library level, independently of the verifier's `asyncio.Lock`-guarded cooldown. The plan correctly distinguishes 'lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard', but does not address the scenario where `lifespan` expires and multiple concurrent `asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt())` calls all hit the stale cache simultaneously. In that scenario PyJWKClient's internal threading lock arbitrates between concurrent threads, but because each call is a separate `to_thread` thread, PyJWKClient may issue one refetch per concurrent thread (depending on its implementation), bypassing the verifier's per-cooldown-window single-fetch guarantee.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'PyJWKClient's `lifespan` is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard'
scenarios: Under high concurrency and a freshly expired lifespan TTL, multiple asyncio.to_thread calls each enter PyJWKClient's fetch path — the verifier's asyncio.Lock is not held at this point, so the flood guard does not apply to lifespan-triggered refetches.

An attacker cannot directly control lifespan expiry, so this is primarily a correctness/reliability issue rather than a security vulnerability, but it means the '≤1 fetch per cooldown window' guarantee stated in the Security section is weaker than claimed.
findings.suggested_fix: Set `PyJWKClient(uri, lifespan=0)` to disable the library's TTL-driven auto-refetch, making the verifier's own cooldown the sole refetch trigger. Add a sentence in the Approach section documenting this choice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the 'same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses' reference in the Approach section — if JWTVerifier does not exist in the installed mcp SDK, replace it with the actual interface the verifier must implement (e.g., the S2 `verify_token` seam contract) as the shape-defining reference.
evidence: Plan text: 'giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam. PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp; ... the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
JWTVerifier UNRESOLVED in mcp, mcp.server.fastmcp, mcp.server.auth in the installed environment.
The core positive rationale (PyJWT is established, already a transitive dep, small module) stands independently without this reference.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as a design-validation reference for the PyJWT approach. `JWTVerifier` is unresolvable in all installed `mcp`/`mcp.server.fastmcp`/`mcp.server.auth` namespaces. If this class does not exist in the installed SDK version, the design-validation claim cannot be verified by an implementer or reviewer, and it may describe a future or removed FastMCP feature — making it misleading prior art rather than a codebase-grounded justification.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: Implementer searches for FastMCP's JWTVerifier to understand the expected interface shape and cannot find it, causing confusion about what the plan means.
The reference describes a future FastMCP feature not yet released, silently coupling the plan to an unverified interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `JWTVerifier` (or its equivalent) exists in the installed `mcp`/FastMCP SDK and, if not, replace the 'same shape as FastMCP's JWTVerifier' prior-art reference with the actual SDK type/interface the JWKSTokenVerifier should conform to.
evidence: Plan quote: 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses, giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', module='mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', module='mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', module='mcp') → UNRESOLVED
Multiple cached review entries (.rebar/cache/plan-review/…) independently flag this as unresolvable
findings.finding: The plan cites 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses' as design-validation prior art to justify the chosen architecture, but JWTVerifier is UNRESOLVED in all probed mcp namespaces (mcp, mcp.server.fastmcp, mcp.server.auth). If JWTVerifier does not exist in the installed mcp SDK, the prior-art reference is vacuous and the executor has no seam to compare against or derive confidence from.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: Executor searches for JWTVerifier in the mcp SDK as a reference implementation and finds nothing, making the design-validation prior-art reference misleading
The SDK may have a different JWT verification surface whose interface differs from what the plan assumes
findings.suggested_fix: Search the installed mcp package (e.g. `grep -r JWTVerifier $(python -c 'import mcp; print(mcp.__file__[:-12])')`) to find the real class name. If absent, remove the reference and instead cite the abstract S2 verifier seam (e.g. `AccessTokenVerifier` or equivalent base class) as the interface anchor.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the cooldown timestamp is updated when a JWKS fetch fails. This leaves an unresolved edge case: if the timestamp IS updated on failure, a single transient JWKS-endpoint error will block all token validation for the entire cooldown window (availability DoS from infrastructure hiccup); if it is NOT updated, an attacker flooding unknown kids that cause fetch failures can trigger unbounded JWKS fetches (the flood-guard goal fails on error paths). The correct behavior is non-obvious and materially affects both security and availability.
findings.location: Security — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' / 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises'
findings.suggested_fix: Update on failure (record the timestamp even when the fetch fails) to preserve the flood-guard property; then document this explicitly so operators understand a transient JWKS outage can extend the rejection window up to one cooldown period.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether a failed JWKS fetch updates the cooldown timestamp. If it does, a single transient JWKS-endpoint error sets the last-refetch mark, blocking all retry attempts for the full cooldown window (default 30 s) while every JWT-authenticated request is denied — an inadvertent denial-of-service on a transient error.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS' + 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises'
findings.suggested_fix: In the cooldown logic: only update `_last_refetch_at` after a successful JWKS fetch. If the fetch raises, propagate the exception without touching the timestamp, so the next request can attempt a refetch immediately.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens to the cooldown timestamp when a JWKS fetch FAILS (raises). If the timestamp IS updated on failure, an attacker flooding one unknown kid can lock out legitimate key-rotation refetches for 30 s; if it is NOT updated, a flood of failing requests (e.g., JWKS endpoint down) can each trigger a new blocked-thread attempt every request cycle, defeating the DoS guard.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)'; Acceptance Criteria — unknown-kid cooldown test
findings.suggested_fix: Recommended policy: update the cooldown timestamp on fetch failure (rate-limit failed fetches the same as successful ones), and document that legitimate rotation after an outage will be picked up on the next post-cooldown request. This keeps the DoS guard intact at the cost of a maximum 30 s recovery delay.

findings.finding: The Approach section justifies the implementation shape by asserting it uses 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' — but `JWTVerifier` is unresolvable in every probed mcp namespace (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`, `mcp.server.fastmcp.server`, `mcp.server.auth.handlers`) and no grep anywhere in the repo finds the string 'JWTVerifier'. This is an empirically-checkable codebase/SDK assertion that cannot be verified.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'

findings.finding: The plan cites 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as design-validation prior art for the chosen `PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode` approach. `JWTVerifier` is UNRESOLVED in every reachable `mcp` namespace (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`). If the class does not exist in the installed SDK the citation is baseless, the design lacks its stated prior-art grounding, and any structural assumptions borrowed from a nonexistent reference may be incorrect.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the three key design decisions — `asyncio.Lock`-guarded cooldown (TOCTOU rationale), `asyncio.to_thread` offload (blocking-fetch rationale), and timestamp-on-failure policy (flood-guard bypass rationale) — so the repo preserves this context independently of the plan ticket.
evidence: Plan's Security section contains detailed rationale for Lock-guarded cooldown and timestamp-on-failure policy but does not commit to persisting this in any repo artifact.
Plan's Approach & Rationale section explains asyncio.to_thread necessity but does not mention an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry.
ABSENCE: no AC item, no plan section, and no mention anywhere of 'ADR', 'CLAUDE.md', or 'design doc' for these decisions.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decisions introduced by this plan — the `asyncio.Lock`-guarded JWKS refetch cooldown, `asyncio.to_thread` offload for the blocking JWKS fetch, and the timestamp-on-failure flood-guard policy — are explained only in the plan ticket. No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or in-repo design note is called out, so these design rationales will be invisible to future maintainers once the ticket is archived.
findings.location: Security section; Approach & rationale section; no ADR/CLAUDE.md update mentioned anywhere in the plan
scenarios: A future maintainer refactors `verify_token` and removes the `asyncio.Lock` not realizing it guards a TOCTOU hazard, reintroducing the flood-guard bypass.
A reviewer questions why `asyncio.to_thread` is used instead of a native async HTTP client and replaces it, breaking the cooldown atomicity model.
The timestamp-on-failure policy is changed to timestamp-on-success during a 'cleanup' pass, silently re-enabling the DoS vector.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0003-jwks-async-cooldown.md`) or a `## Design notes` block in `CLAUDE.md` covering: (1) why `asyncio.to_thread` is required (PyJWKClient is synchronous; calling it directly blocks the event loop), (2) why the cooldown uses an `asyncio.Lock` rather than a simple timestamp check (TOCTOU under concurrent coroutines), and (3) the timestamp-on-failure policy and its bounded cost. Reference this from the code with a comment.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
